_meta.issue: #171 Rebuild ścieżek — P1 AI w domu (formatywna)
_meta.interaction: Klasyfikacja: zadanie dla asystenta vs lepiej zrobić samemu (kind: classify)
_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE przykłady SYNTETYCZNE (NotkaApp, przyklad.test, Jan Syntetyczny, Anna Testowa). ZERO realnych danych/PII. Moduł formatywny — bez progu zaliczenia i certyfikatu.

intro: You already have AI in your pocket: in your phone, camera, translator, and the speaker in your living room. This module shows which of these features genuinely make life easier and which are a five-minute gadget — and how to start using them without turning your home upside down.

screens.title: The voice assistant: what it is good for

blocks.text: A voice assistant works best for short tasks with a clear outcome that you do with your hands full: a timer while cooking, a reminder, a message, controlling the lights or the music. It does worse with long, multi-step matters that require judgment along the way.

items: Great: “set a timer for 12 minutes”, “remind me about my medication tomorrow at 8”, “add milk to the shopping list”.
Good: quick questions about the weather, a route, opening hours — with the habit of verifying important answers.
Weak: “plan my kitchen renovation” — too many decisions and too much context for one voice command.

blocks.title: Rule of thumb
blocks.text: If the task would fit on a sticky note — it is a job for the assistant. If it needs a conversation — sit down with an AI chat, where you can see and refine the answers.

screens.title: AI in your camera, photos, and translator

blocks.text: Most of the quiet, useful AI lives in your phone. It does not look like a chat, but it gets the job done:

items: Camera: night mode, removing accidental passersby from the frame, sharpening old photos.
Photo gallery: search by content — type “receipt”, “dog”, “beach” instead of scrolling through thousands of thumbnails.
Translator: live conversation and translating text through the camera — a menu, a timetable, a medicine label on vacation.
Dictation and transcription: speak instead of typing; a recording of a family planning session turns into a note.

blocks.title: Where to start
blocks.text: Pick ONE feature from this list and use it for a week. A habit built on one feature teaches you more than a tour of twenty.

screens.title: The smart home: value or gadget

blocks.text: At home, AI can be genuinely helpful — and it can be a toy gathering dust in a drawer after a month. The difference usually comes down to one thing: does the device solve a problem you actually have.

rows: A thermostat that learns your daily rhythm
Lights on a schedule and voice command
everyday convenience, also for older people
A smoke/water sensor that alerts your phone
safety, works when you are away
A fridge with a screen suggesting recipes
your phone does the same, without replacing the fridge
A kettle controlled from an app
you still have to get up to pour the water
blocks.textAlt: Table: real value comes from devices that save money or time or increase safety (thermostat, lights, sensors); gadgets tend to be devices that duplicate your phone, like a fridge with a screen or a remote-controlled kettle.

blocks.title: Before you buy
blocks.text: Ask: what problem of MINE does this solve? If the answer starts with “it would be nice to…”, it is a gadget. If it starts with “finally I will not have to…”, it is value.
Devices with a microphone and camera are a convenience that requires two minutes of common sense at setup. We will cover this in detail in the privacy module — here is the minimal version:

items: Find the physical microphone switch on your speaker — and use it whenever you want.
Do not put devices with a camera in the bedroom or in a child’s room.
Every so often, review the assistant’s recording history in the app and clear it.

interaction.title: A task for the assistant, or better done yourself?
interaction.intro: Decide which tasks are worth handing to a voice assistant or the AI in your phone, and which are better done yourself. The feedback explains why.

categories.label: A task for the assistant
categories.desc: Short, a clear outcome, a low cost of error — AI will do it faster.

categories.label: Better done yourself
categories.desc: Requires judgment or trust, or concerns matters where an error costs you.

items.text: Setting a timer for the dumplings when your hands are covered in flour.
items.rationale: A short command, a clear outcome, zero risk — perfect for voice.

items.text: Choosing a loan offer based on a single answer from the assistant.
items.rationale: A financial decision requires comparing offers and verifying — AI can help gather the data, but not decide.

items.text: Finding a photo of a receipt in your gallery by typing the word “receipt”.
items.rationale: Searching by content is a strength of AI in the gallery — seconds instead of scrolling.

items.text: Translating a menu in a foreign restaurant with your phone’s camera.
items.rationale: Live translation is a mature feature; the cost of a mistake with a menu is low.

items.text: Writing personal wedding wishes for your closest friend.
items.rationale: The value of the wishes is their personal character — AI can suggest a draft, but the words should be yours.

items.text: Adding “buy milk and butter” to the shopping list on the way to the car.
items.rationale: A quick voice note — exactly what the assistant was made for.

items.text: Judging whether an e-mail from the bank asking for your details is a scam.
items.rationale: Security requires your own vigilance and verification with the bank — do not delegate it to a machine.

items.text: Turning a recording of a family vacation planning session into a note with a list of decisions.
items.rationale: Transcription and summarizing are AI strengths — and you will review the note anyway.

points: Voice assistant = sticky-note tasks; conversations and decisions = a chat where you can see the answers.
You already have most of the useful AI: gallery, translator, dictation — pick one feature and build the habit.
When shopping for your home, ask about YOUR problem to solve; set up microphones and cameras thoughtfully.
checklist: Do I know where content-based photo search is in my phone?
Have I tried live camera translation?
Do I know how to turn off the microphone on my home speaker?
summary.checklistTitle: Checklist — AI in your phone and home
summary.nextStep: Next step: the module on creating with AI — images, music, and video for family occasions.
